<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17231526]] Wikidata name: Kōga Saburō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese folklore character}} {{nihongo|'''Kōga Saburō'''|甲賀三郎}} is a character in [[Japanese folklore]] associated with the [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]] region.<ref name="Blacker2004">{{cite book|author=Carmen Blacker|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mR6PAgAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA134|date=2 August 2004|publisher=Routledge|isbn=1-135-31872-7}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] on the border of the municipalities of [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino]] and [[:en:Tateshina, Nagano|Tateshina]] in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]].]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>
The third son of a local [[:en:Jitō|landlord]] of [[:en:Kōka District, Shiga|Kōka District]] in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], a distinguished warrior named Kōga Saburō Yorikata (甲賀三郎諏方) was searching for his lost wife, Princess Kasuga (春日姫 ''Kasuga-hime'') in a cave in [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] in [[:en:Shinano, Nagano|Shinano]], with his two elder brothers. The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. After [[Katabasis#Trip into the underworld|travelling through these subterranean lands for a long period of time]], he finally finds his way back to the surface, only to find himself transformed into a giant snake or dragon. With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via [[:en:Folk etymology|Folk etymology]] as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as [[:en:Gokenin|retainers]]. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>[[:en:Richard Dorson|Dorson, Richard M.]] "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''[[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]]'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}

Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''[[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]]'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}

[[en:Kōga Saburō]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]] [[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] {{wikidata link|Q17231526}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17231526]] Wikidata name: Kōga Saburō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese folklore character}} {{nihongo|'''Kōga Saburō'''|甲賀三郎}} is a character in [[Japanese folklore]] associated with the [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]] region.<ref name="Blacker2004">{{cite book|author=Carmen Blacker|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mR6PAgAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA134|date=2 August 2004|publisher=Routledge|isbn=1-135-31872-7}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] on the border of the municipalities of [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino]] and [[:en:Tateshina, Nagano|Tateshina]] in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]].]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>
The third son of a local [[:en:Jitō|landlord]] of [[:en:Kōka District, Shiga|Kōka District]] in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], a distinguished warrior named Kōga Saburō Yorikata (甲賀三郎諏方) was searching for his lost wife, Princess Kasuga (春日姫 ''Kasuga-hime'') in a cave in [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] in [[:en:Shinano, Nagano|Shinano]], with his two elder brothers. The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. After [[Katabasis#Trip into the underworld|travelling through these subterranean lands for a long period of time]], he finally finds his way back to the surface, only to find himself transformed into a giant snake or dragon. With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via [[:en:Folk etymology|Folk etymology]] as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as [[:en:Gokenin|retainers]]. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>[[:en:Richard Dorson|Dorson, Richard M.]] "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''[[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]]'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}

With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via [[:en:Folk etymology|Folk etymology]] as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as [[:en:Gokenin|retainers]]. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>[[:en:Richard Dorson|Dorson, Richard M.]] "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''[[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]]'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17231526]] Wikidata name: Kōga Saburō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese folklore character}} {{nihongo|'''Kōga Saburō'''|甲賀三郎}} is a character in [[Japanese folklore]] associated with the [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]] region.<ref name="Blacker2004">{{cite book|author=Carmen Blacker|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mR6PAgAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA134|date=2 August 2004|publisher=Routledge|isbn=1-135-31872-7}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] on the border of the municipalities of [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino]] and [[:en:Tateshina, Nagano|Tateshina]] in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]].]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>
The third son of a local [[:en:Jitō|landlord]] of [[:en:Kōka District, Shiga|Kōka District]] in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], a distinguished warrior named Kōga Saburō Yorikata (甲賀三郎諏方) was searching for his lost wife, Princess Kasuga (春日姫 ''Kasuga-hime'') in a cave in [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] in [[:en:Shinano, Nagano|Shinano]], with his two elder brothers. The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. After [[Katabasis#Trip into the underworld|travelling through these subterranean lands for a long period of time]], he finally finds his way back to the surface, only to find himself transformed into a giant snake or dragon. With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via [[:en:Folk etymology|Folk etymology]] as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as [[:en:Gokenin|retainers]]. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>[[:en:Richard Dorson|Dorson, Richard M.]] "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''[[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]]'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17231526]] Wikidata name: Kōga Saburō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese folklore character}} {{nihongo|'''Kōga Saburō'''|甲賀三郎}} is a character in [[Japanese folklore]] associated with the [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]] region.<ref name="Blacker2004">{{cite book|author=Carmen Blacker|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mR6PAgAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA134|date=2 August 2004|publisher=Routledge|isbn=1-135-31872-7}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] on the border of the municipalities of [[:en:Chino, Nagano|Chino]] and [[:en:Tateshina, Nagano|Tateshina]] in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]].]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>
The third son of a local [[:en:Jitō|landlord]] of [[:en:Kōka District, Shiga|Kōka District]] in [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], a distinguished warrior named Kōga Saburō Yorikata (甲賀三郎諏方) was searching for his lost wife, Princess Kasuga (春日姫 ''Kasuga-hime'') in a cave in [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] in [[:en:Shinano, Nagano|Shinano]], with his two elder brothers. The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. After [[Katabasis#Trip into the underworld|travelling through these subterranean lands for a long period of time]], he finally finds his way back to the surface, only to find himself transformed into a giant snake or dragon. With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via [[:en:Folk etymology|Folk etymology]] as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]], which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as [[:en:Gokenin|retainers]]. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>[[:en:Richard Dorson|Dorson, Richard M.]] "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''[[:en:Kabuki|Kabuki]]'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}

This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via [[:en:Folk etymology|Folk etymology]] as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>

''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>[[:en:Richard Dorson|Dorson, Richard M.]] "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>

Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via {{ill|Folk etymology|en|Folk etymology|WD=Q109964|lt=Folk etymology}} as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=retainers}}. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>{{ill|Richard Dorson|en|Richard Dorson|WD=Q3352534|lt=Dorson, Richard M.}} "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''{{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17231526]] Wikidata name: Kōga Saburō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese folklore character}} {{nihongo|'''Kōga Saburō'''|甲賀三郎}} is a character in [[Japanese folklore]] associated with the {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa}} region.<ref name="Blacker2004">{{cite book|author=Carmen Blacker|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mR6PAgAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA134|date=2 August 2004|publisher=Routledge|isbn=1-135-31872-7}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} on the border of the municipalities of {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino}} and {{ill|Tateshina, Nagano|en|Tateshina, Nagano|WD=Q1346895|lt=Tateshina}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}.]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>
The third son of a local {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=landlord}} of {{ill|Kōka District, Shiga|en|Kōka District, Shiga|WD=Q4227244|lt=Kōka District}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}, a distinguished warrior named Kōga Saburō Yorikata (甲賀三郎諏方) was searching for his lost wife, Princess Kasuga (春日姫 ''Kasuga-hime'') in a cave in {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} in {{ill|Shinano, Nagano|en|Shinano, Nagano|WD=Q1349050|lt=Shinano}}, with his two elder brothers. The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. After [[Katabasis#Trip into the underworld|travelling through these subterranean lands for a long period of time]], he finally finds his way back to the surface, only to find himself transformed into a giant snake or dragon. With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via {{ill|Folk etymology|en|Folk etymology|WD=Q109964|lt=Folk etymology}} as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=retainers}}. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>{{ill|Richard Dorson|en|Richard Dorson|WD=Q3352534|lt=Dorson, Richard M.}} "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''{{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}

In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''{{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}

[[en:Kōga Saburō]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]] [[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] {{wikidata link|Q17231526}} [[Category:Japanese dragons]] [[Category:Japanese legends]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kōga Saburō]][[ja:甲賀三郎 (伝説)]][[zh:甲賀三郎 (傳說)]]
{{Short description|Japanese folklore character}} {{nihongo|'''Kōga Saburō'''|甲賀三郎}} is a character in [[Japanese folklore]] associated with the {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa}} region.<ref name="Blacker2004">{{cite book|author=Carmen Blacker|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mR6PAgAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA134|date=2 August 2004|publisher=Routledge|isbn=1-135-31872-7}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} on the border of the municipalities of {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino}} and {{ill|Tateshina, Nagano|en|Tateshina, Nagano|WD=Q1346895|lt=Tateshina}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}.]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>
The third son of a local {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=landlord}} of {{ill|Kōka District, Shiga|en|Kōka District, Shiga|WD=Q4227244|lt=Kōka District}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}, a distinguished warrior named Kōga Saburō Yorikata (甲賀三郎諏方) was searching for his lost wife, Princess Kasuga (春日姫 ''Kasuga-hime'') in a cave in {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} in {{ill|Shinano, Nagano|en|Shinano, Nagano|WD=Q1349050|lt=Shinano}}, with his two elder brothers. The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. After [[Katabasis#Trip into the underworld|travelling through these subterranean lands for a long period of time]], he finally finds his way back to the surface, only to find himself transformed into a giant snake or dragon. With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via {{ill|Folk etymology|en|Folk etymology|WD=Q109964|lt=Folk etymology}} as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}, which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=retainers}}. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>{{ill|Richard Dorson|en|Richard Dorson|WD=Q3352534|lt=Dorson, Richard M.}} "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''{{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}}'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}
{{Suwa Faith}}

{{Short description|Japanese folklore character}} {{nihongo|'''Kōga Saburō'''|甲賀三郎}} is a character in [[Japanese folklore]] associated with the {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa|1=Suwa, Nagano}} region.<ref name="Blacker2004">{{cite book|author=Carmen Blacker|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mR6PAgAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA134|date=2 August 2004|publisher=Routledge|isbn=1-135-31872-7}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina|1=Mount Tateshina}} on the border of the municipalities of {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|WD=Q838660|lt=Chino|1=Chino, Nagano}} and {{ill|Tateshina, Nagano|en|Tateshina, Nagano|WD=Q1346895|lt=Tateshina|1=Tateshina, Nagano}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}.]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>
The third son of a local {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=landlord|1=Jitō}} of {{ill|Kōka District, Shiga|en|Kōka District, Shiga|WD=Q4227244|lt=Kōka District|1=Kōka District, Shiga}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}, a distinguished warrior named Kōga Saburō Yorikata (甲賀三郎諏方) was searching for his lost wife, Princess Kasuga (春日姫 ''Kasuga-hime'') in a cave in {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina|1=Mount Tateshina}} in {{ill|Shinano, Nagano|en|Shinano, Nagano|WD=Q1349050|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano, Nagano}}, with his two elder brothers. The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. After [[Katabasis#Trip into the underworld|travelling through these subterranean lands for a long period of time]], he finally finds his way back to the surface, only to find himself transformed into a giant snake or dragon. With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via {{ill|Folk etymology|en|Folk etymology|WD=Q109964|lt=Folk etymology|1=Folk etymology}} as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Hōjō clan}}, which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=retainers|1=Gokenin}}. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>{{ill|Richard Dorson|en|Richard Dorson|WD=Q3352534|lt=Dorson, Richard M.|1=Richard Dorson}} "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''{{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}}'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}
{{Suwa Faith}}

{{Short description|Japanese folklore character}} {{nihongo|'''Kōga Saburō'''|甲賀三郎}} is a character in [[Japanese folklore]] associated with the {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|qid=Q846338|lt=Suwa|1=Suwa, Nagano}} region.<ref name="Blacker2004">{{cite book|author=Carmen Blacker|title=The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mR6PAgAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA134|date=2 August 2004|publisher=Routledge|isbn=1-135-31872-7}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|qid=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina|1=Mount Tateshina}} on the border of the municipalities of {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|qid=Q838660|lt=Chino|1=Chino, Nagano}} and {{ill|Tateshina, Nagano|en|Tateshina, Nagano|qid=Q1346895|lt=Tateshina|1=Tateshina, Nagano}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}.]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>
The third son of a local {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|qid=Q1329805|lt=landlord|1=Jitō}} of {{ill|Kōka District, Shiga|en|Kōka District, Shiga|qid=Q4227244|lt=Kōka District|1=Kōka District, Shiga}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}, a distinguished warrior named Kōga Saburō Yorikata (甲賀三郎諏方) was searching for his lost wife, Princess Kasuga (春日姫 ''Kasuga-hime'') in a cave in {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|qid=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina|1=Mount Tateshina}} in {{ill|Shinano, Nagano|en|Shinano, Nagano|qid=Q1349050|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano, Nagano}}, with his two elder brothers. The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. After [[Katabasis#Trip into the underworld|travelling through these subterranean lands for a long period of time]], he finally finds his way back to the surface, only to find himself transformed into a giant snake or dragon. With the help of Buddhist monks (who turn out to be gods in disguise), Saburō regains his human form and is finally reunited with his wife. Saburō eventually becomes [[Takeminakata|Suwa Myōjin]], the god of the Upper Shrine of Suwa, while his wife becomes the goddess of the Lower Shrine.<ref name="SuwaEngi" />
This version of the legend explains the origin of the name 'Suwa' (諏訪 or 諏方) via {{ill|Folk etymology|en|Folk etymology|qid=Q109964|lt=Folk etymology|1=Folk etymology}} as being derived from Saburō's personal name, Yorikata (諏方).<ref>Kishi (trans.) (1967). p. 282.</ref>
==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|qid=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Hōjō clan}}, which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|qid=Q63765|lt=retainers|1=Gokenin}}. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>{{ill|Richard Dorson|en|Richard Dorson|qid=Q3352534|lt=Dorson, Richard M.|1=Richard Dorson}} "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
There are a number of variants on the basic tale; some see Kōga reunited with his wife (and suggest that they had a son together in the underworld),<ref name="Blacker2004"/> some involve his two brothers joining him on the search,<ref name="Dorson2012">{{cite book|author=Richard M. Dorson|title=Folk Legends of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BkTRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT145|date=11 September 2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0963-6|page=145}}</ref> others include him fighting against a resident serpent god before becoming one himself.<ref>{{cite book|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA98|year=1964|publisher=Brill Archive|page=98|id=GGKEY:2TUFXDJBJPN}}</ref>
The story of Kōga Saburō was adapted into a ''{{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|qid=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}}'' play, ''Kōga Saburō in the Cave'' in around 1808.<ref name="Dorson2012"/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== *{{cite book|editor1-last=Fukuda|editor1-first=Akira|editor2-last=Nihonmatsu|editor2-first=Yasuhiro|editor3-last=Tokuda|editor3-first=Kazuo|title=諏訪信仰の中世―神話・伝承・歴史 (Suwa shinkō no chūsei: shinwa, denshō, rekishi)|date=2015|publisher=Miyai Shoten|isbn=978-4838232888}} {{in lang|ja}} *{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Tenbi|title=諏訪信仰史 (Suwa shinkōshi)|date=1982|publisher=Meicho Shuppan|isbn=978-4626001245}} {{in lang|ja}}

==Summary== [[File:Tateshina03.JPG|thumb|right|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|qid=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina|1=Mount Tateshina}} on the border of the municipalities of {{ill|Chino, Nagano|en|Chino, Nagano|qid=Q838660|lt=Chino|1=Chino, Nagano}} and {{ill|Tateshina, Nagano|en|Tateshina, Nagano|qid=Q1346895|lt=Tateshina|1=Tateshina, Nagano}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}.]] Many variants on the basic story exist; the following summary is based on the earliest literary version of the tale found in the ''[[:en:Shintoshu|Shintōshū]]''.<ref name="SuwaEngi">諏訪縁起の事 (''Suwa engi-no-koto'') in {{cite book|author=Kishi, Shōzō (trans.)|title=Shintōshū (神道集)|series=Tōyō Bunko (東洋文庫) vol. 94|date=1967|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=978-4-582-80094-4|pages=238–286}}</ref><ref name="Kanai17">Kanai (1982). p. 17.</ref>

The second brother, who was jealous of Saburō's prowess and fame and who coveted Kasuga, traps the latter inside the cave after they had rescued the princess.
With no way out, Saburō has no choice but to go deeper into the cave, which was actually an entrance to various underground realms filled with many wonders. ==Origins== It has been proposed that the Kōga Saburō legend arose as a result of the collapse of the Kamakura shogunate and the downfall of the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|qid=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Hōjō clan}}, which the [[Suwa clan]], the high priestly house of the Upper Suwa Shrine, had faithfully served as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|qid=Q63765|lt=retainers|1=Gokenin}}. The status and prestige of the clan and the shrine's high priest, the ''Ōhōri'' (大祝), revered as [[Arahitogami|a living god]], having been diminished in the aftermath of these events, localized stories about the shrine's deity such as that of Kōga Saburō began to be favored and circulate in various areas.<ref>Fukuda; Nihonmatsu; Tokuda, eds. (2015). pp. 130-132.</ref>
==Variants== Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, in the Japanese tale index, as type 301A, "Kooga Saburoo's Journey" (''Kooga Saburoo'').<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=%22301A%22 A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 70.</ref><ref>{{ill|Richard Dorson|en|Richard Dorson|qid=Q3352534|lt=Dorson, Richard M.|1=Richard Dorson}} "[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.39000002538523&view=1up&seq=158&skin=2021 National Characteristics of Japanese Folktales]". In: ''Folklore, Nationalism and Politics''. Edited by Felix J. Oinas. Indiana University Folklore Institute Monograph Series, Vol. 30. Columbus, Ohio: Slavica Publishers, 1978. p. 156.</ref>
